title: Kubernetes 101 — Codespaces & DevContainer Workflow
description: Test your knowledge of the GitHub Codespaces development workflow used in this enablement: secrets, devcontainer lifecycle, MkDocs publishing, and port management.
imagine: You are a Dynatrace Solutions Engineer building your first enablement lab using the Codespaces framework. You have to make decisions about secrets, documentation publishing, and port visibility.
yourGoal: Answer five questions that cover the operational workflow of building and running enablements inside GitHub Codespaces with the Dynatrace Codespaces Framework.

story.introduction: The Dynatrace Codespaces Framework automates Kubernetes cluster setup, app deployment, and documentation publishing. Understanding its lifecycle is key to building reliable labs.
story.context: Answers can be found in the lab pages covering Getting Started, Codespaces, and Content.

questions.title: Codespace secrets
questions.content: When launching this Codespace, you configure three secrets: DT_ENVIRONMENT, DT_OPERATOR_TOKEN, and DT_INGEST_TOKEN. Where are these secrets stored and injected from?

options.text: They are hardcoded in the devcontainer.json file
options.explanation: Hardcoding secrets in config files would be a security risk — they'd be visible in the repo.

options.text: They are stored in GitHub Codespaces encrypted secrets and injected as environment variables at launch
options.explanation: Correct! GitHub encrypts Codespace secrets and makes them available as env vars inside the container.

options.text: They are fetched from the Dynatrace API at container startup
options.explanation: The container needs the DT_ENVIRONMENT URL to talk to Dynatrace in the first place.

options.text: They are stored in a .env file committed to the repository
options.explanation: Committing secrets to a repo is a security anti-pattern and the framework never does this.

questions.explanation: GitHub Codespaces secrets are per-user encrypted values that are injected as environment variables when the container starts. This keeps them out of source control.
hints: The lab says to 'enter your credentials within the following variables' when creating the Codespace.
Think about where GitHub stores user-specific config that is NOT in the repo.

questions.title: DevContainer lifecycle hooks
questions.content: The framework uses `post-create.sh` and `post-start.sh` scripts. When does `post-create.sh` execute?

options.text: Every time the Codespace is resumed from a stopped state
options.explanation: That's when post-start.sh runs. post-create runs only once.

options.text: Only once, when the container is first created (not on subsequent starts)
options.explanation: Correct! postCreateCommand runs exactly once after the initial container build.

options.text: Manually, when the user types 'post-create' in the terminal
options.explanation: It's triggered automatically by the devcontainer lifecycle, not invoked manually.

options.text: During the Docker image build phase, before the container starts
options.explanation: That would be a Dockerfile RUN step. postCreateCommand runs after the container is running.

questions.explanation: The devcontainer lifecycle has distinct hooks: postCreateCommand (once on creation), postStartCommand (on each start), and postAttachCommand (on each VS Code attach).
hints: Think about what 'create' means in the Codespace lifecycle vs 'start'.
post-create is for one-time setup (install tools, deploy apps); post-start is for recurring tasks.

questions.title: MkDocs RUM integration
questions.content: Each markdown page includes a JavaScript snippet (e.g., `--8<-- "snippets/index.js"`). Why is this required?

options.text: It enables syntax highlighting for code blocks in MkDocs
options.explanation: MkDocs Material handles syntax highlighting via its own extensions, not per-page JS snippets.

options.text: It injects Dynatrace Real User Monitoring (agentless RUM) to track page engagement and time-on-page
options.explanation: Correct! The framework uses agentless RUM to understand which lab pages users spend time on.

options.text: It imports the MkDocs Material theme for each page
options.explanation: The theme is configured once in mkdocs.yaml, not imported per page.

options.text: It provides client-side search functionality
options.explanation: MkDocs search is built into the theme and doesn't need per-page JS includes.

questions.explanation: Because the labs use agentless RUM, each page needs a JS snippet to send user interaction data to Dynatrace. The snippet name matches the nav entry for consistency in analytics.
hints: The lab explains we want to 'monitor the usage and adoption of the Github pages'.
Since it's agentless RUM, it can't be auto-injected — it must be explicitly added.

questions.title: Publishing GitHub Pages
questions.content: The `deployGhdocs` shell function publishes the documentation. What branch does it push the built site to?

options.text: main
options.explanation: main contains the source; publishing to it would mix source and output.

options.text: gh-pages
options.explanation: Correct! MkDocs builds the site and pushes the HTML to the gh-pages branch, which GitHub Pages serves.

options.text: docs
options.explanation: The 'docs' folder is a source directory, not a branch for published output.

options.text: release
options.explanation: There is no 'release' branch in this workflow.

questions.explanation: MkDocs' `gh-deploy` command builds the site from source, then force-pushes the output to the gh-pages branch. GitHub Pages then serves the content from that branch.
hints: Look at the deploy output in the lab — it explicitly says which branch it pushes to.
GitHub Pages typically serves from either a /docs folder on main or a dedicated branch.

questions.title: Making Codespace ports public
questions.content: The lab says ports are exposed locally by default. How do you make an exposed port publicly accessible from outside the Codespace?

options.text: Add a firewall rule in the devcontainer.json file
options.explanation: devcontainer.json can declare forwarded ports but doesn't manage public visibility.

options.text: In VS Code's Ports tab, right-click the port and change visibility to 'Public'
options.explanation: Correct! GitHub Codespaces controls port visibility through the VS Code Ports panel.

options.text: Run 'kubectl expose' with --external flag
options.explanation: kubectl expose creates a Kubernetes Service — it doesn't control Codespace port visibility.

options.text: Push the code to a public repository
options.explanation: Repository visibility and port visibility are completely independent.

questions.explanation: GitHub Codespaces ports default to 'private' (only the Codespace owner can access). Changing to 'public' in the Ports tab makes the forwarded URL accessible to anyone with the link.
hints: The lab page mentions 'go to the ports section in VSCode, right click on them'.
This is a Codespaces platform feature, not a Kubernetes feature.

learningObjectives: Explain how GitHub Codespaces secrets are managed and injected
Distinguish between devcontainer lifecycle hooks (post-create vs post-start)
Describe why per-page JS snippets are needed for agentless RUM in MkDocs
Know the publishing workflow for GitHub Pages via gh-pages branch
Control port visibility in GitHub Codespaces
